q1_conscious.text: Is the patient conscious and able to respond to you?

q_alt_unconscious.text: Patient is unconscious with a suspected airway obstruction. This is a resuscitation situation â€” the unresponsive protocol applies.

q2_can_cough_or_speak.text: Can the patient cough forcefully, speak, or make sounds?

q3_able_to_assist.text: Is there someone at the scene able and willing to perform back blows and abdominal thrusts right now, before the ambulance arrives?
q3_able_to_assist.guidance_note: Technique differs by age group: standard back blows/abdominal thrusts for children over 1 year and adults; back blows and chest thrusts only (no abdominal thrusts) for infants under 1 year due to organ injury risk.

q4_patient_age_group.text: Is the patient an infant under 1 year old, a child, or an adult?

pre_arrival_instructions: The patient can still cough and breathe, which means some air is moving â€” this is a good sign.
Encourage the patient to keep coughing forcefully â€” this is the most effective way to clear the obstruction.
Do not perform back blows or abdominal thrusts while the cough remains effective â€” this could push the object further in or cause injury.
Stay with the patient and watch closely. If the cough becomes weak, silent, or the patient cannot breathe at all, call back immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
If you become able to assist, or someone else arrives, tell me immediately and I will guide you through back blows and abdominal thrusts.
If the patient becomes unconscious at any point, tell me immediately â€” the instructions change.
Stay on the line with me. This is for an infant under 1 year old â€” do not use abdominal thrusts.
Lay the infant face down along your forearm, supporting the head and jaw, head lower than the chest.
Give 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand.
If the object does not come out, turn the infant face up along your other arm, head still lower than chest.
Give 5 chest thrusts using two fingers in the centre of the chest, just below the nipple line.
Repeat back blows and chest thrusts until the object clears or the infant becomes unconscious â€” if that happens, tell me immediately.
Stay on the line with me.
Stand behind the patient and give up to 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand.
If the object does not clear, stand behind them, place a fist just above the navel, grasp it with your other hand, and give up to 5 quick upward abdominal thrusts.
Alternate between 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts until the object clears or the patient becomes unconscious â€” if that happens, tell me immediately, the instructions change to CPR.
The patient is unconscious â€” the choking protocol no longer applies.
Place the patient on their back on a firm surface.
If you are trained, open the airway and check for breathing.
If the patient is not breathing, start CPR immediately.
Help is on the way.
